sectionName: Psychological, Social, & Biological Foundations of Behavior

questions.passage:
Researchers test tactile perception in volunteers using von Frey filaments applied to the fingertip. Each trial presents either one filament or two filaments separated by a controlled distance. Participants report whether they feel one point or two. The aggregate psychometric function for two-point reports is plotted in Figure 1.

A second task measures the just-noticeable difference (JND) for lifted weights.

Weight standard | Average JND
50 g | 2 g
100 g | 4 g
200 g | 8 g

The investigators explain that receptive-field size, lateral inhibition, and cortical magnification affect spatial acuity. They also note that Weber's law predicts a roughly constant ratio between JND and stimulus magnitude for many sensory dimensions.
questions.stem: Based on Figure 1, the absolute two-point threshold for this population is closest to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: 1.0 mm

choices.label: B
choices.text: 2.3 mm

choices.label: C
choices.text: 4.0 mm

choices.label: D
choices.text: 5.0 mm

explanations.A: At 1.0 mm, the curve is still well below the 50% criterion, so most participants report one point rather than two. This value underestimates the threshold by reading too far left on the psychometric function.
explanations.B: Correct. The absolute two-point threshold is defined here as the separation producing 50% “two points” reports. Tracing horizontally from 50% on the y-axis to the psychometric curve and then down to the x-axis gives a value a little above 2 mm, closest to 2.3 mm.
explanations.C: At 4.0 mm, the curve is already high on the psychometric function, meaning most trials are reported as two points. That separation is above threshold rather than the point of transition.
explanations.D: At 5.0 mm, performance is near ceiling, so it does not identify the threshold. Psychophysical thresholds are read near the midpoint of the response curve, not where the task has become easy.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Two-point discrimination threshold testing on the fingertip. Data points show percentage of trials reported as "two points" at each caliper separation.

questions.stem: Which finding best illustrates Weber's law in the weight task?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The JND is always exactly 2 g.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Participants cannot compare weights.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The JND decreases as weight increases.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The JND-to-standard ratio remains about 0.04.

explanations.A: The absolute JND changes from 2 g to 4 g to 8 g as the standard increases. Weber's law is not about a constant absolute difference; it is about a roughly constant proportional difference.
explanations.B: Participants can compare weights because JND values are measured. The table quantifies the difference needed for reliable discrimination; it does not show absence of comparison ability.
explanations.C: The JND increases, not decreases, with larger standards. That increase is not a violation of Weber's law because the proportional ratio remains stable. The distractor confuses absolute and relative sensitivity.
explanations.D: Correct. The ratios are 2/50, 4/100, and 8/200, each equal to 0.04. This proportional scaling is the key feature of Weber's law and explains why larger stimuli require larger absolute changes to be noticed.

questions.stem: Why does the fingertip have better spatial acuity than the upper arm?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Smaller receptive fields and greater cortical representation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Larger receptive fields and less cortical representation

choices.label: C
choices.text: No lateral inhibition in the fingertip

choices.label: D
choices.text: Complete absence of mechanoreceptors in the arm

explanations.A: Correct. Fingertips have dense mechanoreceptor innervation, smaller receptive fields, and expanded somatosensory cortical representation. These features allow the nervous system to localize nearby stimuli more precisely.
explanations.B: Large receptive fields reduce spatial precision because nearby stimuli activate overlapping neural populations. The upper arm has poorer acuity partly for that reason. This option describes the less sensitive region, not the fingertip.
explanations.C: Lateral inhibition helps sharpen spatial contrast, so its absence would worsen acuity. The fingertip's high acuity is not due to losing lateral inhibition but to dense receptors, small fields, and cortical magnification.
explanations.D: The upper arm does contain mechanoreceptors; it simply has lower receptor density and larger receptive fields than the fingertip. Spatial acuity varies across skin regions rather than being all-or-none.

questions.stem: Lateral inhibition improves tactile localization primarily by:

choices.label: A
choices.text: broadening activity across neighboring neurons.

choices.label: B
choices.text: eliminating all action potentials.

choices.label: C
choices.text: suppressing neighboring responses to sharpen contrast around the most stimulated region.

choices.label: D
choices.text: moving receptors from skin to cortex.

explanations.A: Broadening neighboring activity would blur the representation and reduce localization precision. Lateral inhibition does the opposite: it suppresses adjacent weaker signals so the strongest signal stands out.
explanations.B: Eliminating all action potentials would abolish sensation rather than sharpen it. Lateral inhibition is selective modulation of neighboring pathways, not complete shutdown of the sensory system.
explanations.C: Correct. Lateral inhibition sharpens sensory contrast by reducing activity in neurons surrounding the most active pathway. This makes spatial boundaries more distinct, improving two-point discrimination and localization.
explanations.D: Receptors remain in the skin. Cortical representation can be magnified, but lateral inhibition refers to neural circuit interactions that sharpen signals, not physical movement of receptors.

questions.stem: Which change would most likely lower the two-point threshold?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increasing receptive-field size

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increasing receptor density and cortical representation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Reducing receptor density

choices.label: D
choices.text: Removing all sensory feedback

explanations.A: Larger receptive fields make two nearby points more likely to fall within the same neural territory, raising the threshold. That would worsen acuity rather than lowering the distance needed for discrimination.
explanations.B: Correct. More receptors and greater cortical representation improve spatial resolution, allowing smaller separations to be distinguished. A lower threshold means finer acuity, not weaker sensation.
explanations.C: Lower receptor density provides less spatial sampling and would impair discrimination. The fingertip's low threshold depends on many receptors covering small areas.
explanations.D: Removing sensory feedback would prevent discrimination entirely. The question asks how to improve threshold, so the change must increase useful sensory information rather than eliminate it.

questions.passage:
A vision lab measures dark adaptation after participants leave a brightly lit room. Sensitivity improves slowly over 30 minutes. In a second experiment, participants identify letters flashed briefly at different eccentricities while fixating on a central dot. Performance is best near the fovea for color and fine detail, but peripheral motion detection remains strong.

Time in dark | Detection threshold
0 min | high
5 min | lower
20 min | much lower
30 min | plateau

The researchers discuss rods, cones, photopigment regeneration, signal detection theory, and the distinction between absolute threshold and response bias.
questions.stem: Why does detection threshold decrease during dark adaptation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cones become the only active photoreceptors at 30 minutes.

choices.label: B
choices.text: All rods are destroyed by darkness.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Photopigments regenerate and retinal sensitivity increases.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The optic nerve stops firing.

explanations.A: Cones support color and high acuity in bright conditions, especially at the fovea. Rods dominate much of dark-adapted sensitivity. The 30-minute plateau is not a cone-only state.
explanations.B: Darkness does not destroy rods. Rods become especially important in low-light sensitivity after adaptation. If rods were destroyed, threshold would rise rather than fall.
explanations.C: Correct. After bright light bleaches photopigments, time in darkness allows rhodopsin and other photopigments to regenerate, increasing sensitivity. A lower threshold means less light is needed for detection.
explanations.D: If the optic nerve stopped firing, visual detection would fail. Dark adaptation changes retinal sensitivity and phototransduction readiness, not by silencing all output.

questions.stem: Why is fine color detail best near the fovea?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The fovea has high cone density and small receptive fields.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The fovea contains only rods.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Peripheral retina has no photoreceptors.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Color vision depends on the auditory cortex.

explanations.A: Correct. The fovea is densely packed with cones and has circuitry supporting high spatial acuity. Cones mediate color vision and fine detail under photopic conditions, explaining strong central performance.
explanations.B: The fovea is cone-rich, not rod-only. Rods are more important in peripheral and low-light vision. This reversal would predict poor color acuity at fixation, contradicting the passage.
explanations.C: The peripheral retina has photoreceptors and is useful for motion and low-light detection. Its lower acuity comes from different receptor density and convergence, not absence of receptors.
explanations.D: Color vision is processed through visual pathways, beginning in retinal cones and continuing through visual cortex. The auditory cortex is unrelated to color discrimination.

questions.stem: In signal detection theory, a participant who says “yes” more often without improved sensitivity has changed:

choices.label: A
choices.text: wavelength of all stimuli

choices.label: B
choices.text: photopigment chemistry

choices.label: C
choices.text: retinal eccentricity

choices.label: D
choices.text: response criterion

explanations.A: The wavelength of stimuli is a physical property of the light. A response bias changes reports, not the actual stimulus wavelengths presented by the experimenter.
explanations.B: Photopigment chemistry affects retinal sensitivity, especially in dark adaptation. A mere tendency to answer yes more often is a decision-level shift, not necessarily a change in phototransduction.
explanations.C: Retinal eccentricity concerns where stimuli fall on the retina. The scenario changes response behavior at the same task, not stimulus location. Criterion is the relevant signal-detection concept.
explanations.D: Correct. Signal detection theory separates sensitivity from decision criterion. A liberal response bias increases yes responses, including hits and false alarms, without necessarily improving the sensory ability to distinguish signal from noise.

questions.stem: Which result would indicate improved sensitivity rather than only a liberal response bias?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hits increase and false alarms decrease.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hits increase while false alarms increase equally.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The participant says yes to every trial.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The participant refuses to respond.

explanations.A: Correct. Improved sensitivity means better separation of signal from noise, so hits can rise while false alarms fall. That pattern shows the participant is detecting true signals more accurately rather than merely shifting willingness to say yes.
explanations.B: Equal increases in hits and false alarms suggest a more liberal criterion: the participant says yes more often regardless of whether signal is present. Sensitivity has not clearly improved if discrimination between signal and noise is unchanged.
explanations.C: Saying yes to every trial maximizes hits but also maximizes false alarms. It reflects an extremely liberal criterion, not good discrimination. Signal detection theory was designed to expose this distinction.
explanations.D: Refusing to respond prevents estimation of either sensitivity or criterion. It does not demonstrate perceptual improvement and would make the experiment uninterpretable.

questions.stem: Peripheral motion detection remains strong partly because peripheral vision has:

choices.label: A
choices.text: higher cone density than the fovea for all tasks.

choices.label: B
choices.text: greater convergence and sensitivity to change, though lower acuity.

choices.label: C
choices.text: no rods and no ganglion cells.

choices.label: D
choices.text: exclusive control by taste receptors.

explanations.A: Cone density is highest at the fovea, not the periphery. If the periphery had higher cone density for all tasks, the passage's central advantage for fine color detail would be difficult to explain.
explanations.B: Correct. Peripheral retina has more convergence and is well suited for detecting motion and low-light changes, though spatial detail is poorer. This explains why peripheral awareness can be strong even when central vision dominates fine color detail.
explanations.C: The periphery contains rods and ganglion cells. Its strengths and weaknesses reflect receptor distribution and neural pooling, not absence of retinal circuitry.
explanations.D: Taste receptors do not mediate visual motion detection. This distractor is categorically wrong and ignores the passage's retinal explanation.
